title: Load-Bearing Wall Removal

budget.description: Short wall (6-8 linear ft) with simple load path, standard LVL beam, minimal mechanical rerouting, basic drywall patching

midRange.description: Medium wall (10-14 linear ft) with engineered steel or LVL beam, some electrical/plumbing rerouting, full drywall finish and paint

premium.description: Long wall (16-20+ linear ft) with steel I-beam, significant mechanical rerouting, structural engineering, concealed beam with flush ceiling finish

costFactors.title: Wall Length and Load Above
costFactors.description: A short 6-foot wall supporting only a roof load needs a smaller beam and simpler temporary supports. A 20-foot wall carrying a second floor, roof, and possibly point loads from above requires a much larger beam, heavier temporary shoring, and more labor. Longer walls also mean longer beams, which are harder to maneuver into position and may require a steel I-beam instead of engineered wood.

costFactors.title: Beam Material
costFactors.description: An LVL (laminated veneer lumber) beam for a short span might cost $300-$600 for materials. A steel I-beam for a long span can run $1,000-$3,000 just for the beam itself, plus it requires welding or special connections. Steel beams are heavier and need more labor to install but allow longer spans without intermediate posts.

costFactors.title: Mechanical Systems in the Wall
costFactors.description: Load-bearing walls often contain electrical wiring, plumbing pipes, HVAC ducts, or even gas lines. Rerouting a few electrical circuits costs $300-$800. Moving plumbing drain lines is $500-$1,500. Rerouting HVAC ductwork that runs through the wall can add $800-$2,000. A contractor should open a section of the wall before giving a final price.

costFactors.title: Structural Engineering Fees
costFactors.description: Most jurisdictions require a licensed structural engineer to design the beam and specify post sizes and footings. A simple beam calculation for a short wall costs $300-$500. Complex situations with multiple load paths, second-story loads, or unusual framing may require $800-$1,500 in engineering fees. Never skip this step - an undersized beam can cause serious structural problems.

costFactors.title: Finish Work
costFactors.description: After the beam is installed, you need to patch drywall on the ceiling and adjacent walls, match texture, and paint. A basic patch job costs $500-$800. If you want the beam fully concealed with a flush ceiling, expect $1,200-$2,000 for framing, drywall, and finish work. Some homeowners choose to leave the beam exposed as a design feature, which saves on drywall but may need staining or wrapping ($300-$600).

materialOptions.pros: Affordable, widely available, easy to cut on site, can be concealed in standard ceiling framing
materialOptions.cons: Limited span length (typically up to 16 ft without intermediate support), deeper beam depth than steel
materialOptions.bestFor: Spans under 14 feet in single-story homes or where the beam can be hidden in ceiling framing

materialOptions.pros: Handles the longest spans, thinnest profile for a given load, extremely strong, no deflection issues
materialOptions.cons: Heavy and difficult to install, requires welding or bolted connections, more expensive, may need fireproofing
materialOptions.bestFor: Long spans over 14 feet, multi-story load paths, or situations where minimum beam depth is critical

materialOptions.pros: Attractive for exposed applications, strong, available in long lengths, sustainable material
materialOptions.cons: More expensive than LVL, deeper profile than steel, requires finishing if exposed
materialOptions.bestFor: Open-concept designs where the beam will be left exposed as an architectural feature

materialOptions.pros: Very strong, consistent quality, good for heavy loads, available in large sizes
materialOptions.cons: Heavier than LVL, not attractive if exposed, limited availability in some markets
materialOptions.bestFor: High-load situations where steel is not desired and the beam will be concealed

materialOptions.pros: Invisible after installation, maintains flat ceiling line, cleanest finished appearance
materialOptions.cons: Most expensive option due to extra framing and finish work, may require deeper ceiling modification, not possible with all joist configurations
materialOptions.bestFor: Open-concept living spaces where a visible beam would disrupt the ceiling line

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: Higher labor rates and strict permitting. NYC and Boston metro areas often require expedited engineering reviews that add to costs.

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: Seismic requirements in California often mandate steel beams and more robust connections, adding $1,000-$3,000 to typical costs.

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Lower labor rates and simpler permitting keep costs down. Many SE homes are single-story slab-on-grade, which simplifies the work.

midwest.label: Midwest
midwest.note: Competitive contractor market keeps prices moderate. Basement homes may have existing beam-and-post systems that simplify load path design.

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Snow loads in mountain areas may require larger beams. Growing markets in Denver and Salt Lake City push labor costs slightly above average.

diyVsPro.diyFeasibility: Very Low - This is one of the most dangerous DIY projects a homeowner can attempt. Improper shoring can cause ceiling or floor collapse.
diyVsPro.diySavings: 15-25% if you handle demolition prep and all finish work yourself
diyTasks: Removing drywall from the wall before the contractor arrives
Cleaning up and hauling debris after demolition
Drywall patching and finishing after the beam is installed
Painting the ceiling and walls after repairs
Installing trim or molding around the beam if exposed
proOnlyTasks: Structural engineering calculations and beam sizing
Temporary shoring and load transfer during demolition
Rerouting electrical wiring, plumbing, or HVAC
Verifying the wall is actually load-bearing (vs. partition)
diyVsPro.diyRisks: Removing a load-bearing wall without proper shoring and an engineered beam can cause catastrophic structural failure. Ceilings can sag, floors above can drop, and in worst cases the roof structure can partially collapse. Even if the house does not fail immediately, an undersized beam can cause slow deflection that cracks drywall, misaligns doors, and creates long-term structural damage. This project requires a licensed structural engineer and permitted work in virtually every jurisdiction.

savingsTips: Do your own demolition. Removing drywall and hauling debris before the structural crew arrives can save $300-$600 in labor costs.
Get the structural engineering done first. Having stamped drawings ready when you get contractor bids shows you are serious and lets them price the job accurately instead of padding for unknowns.
Choose an LVL beam over steel if the span allows it. LVL beams cost 50-70% less than steel and are faster to install.
Leave the beam exposed instead of paying for a flush ceiling finish. A stained or painted exposed beam can look great and saves $800-$1,500 in finish work.
Handle all painting and drywall finishing yourself. This is straightforward DIY work that saves $500-$1,000.
Combine this project with other remodeling work. If a contractor is already on site for a kitchen remodel, the wall removal will cost less as a line item than a standalone project.
Check your wall for utilities before committing. Open a small inspection hole to see what is inside. A wall with no plumbing or HVAC runs is much cheaper to remove.
Get at least three bids. Pricing for wall removal varies widely because contractors assess risk differently. Three bids help you find the fair price.

contractorQuestions.question: How will you determine if this wall is load-bearing?
contractorQuestions.why: A competent contractor should explain how they verify load paths - checking the basement or crawl space for beams below, looking at joist direction, and reviewing framing. If they just say 'I can tell by looking,' find someone else.

contractorQuestions.question: Will you provide a stamped structural engineering plan, or do I need to hire the engineer separately?
contractorQuestions.why: Some contractors include engineering in their price while others expect you to provide it. Engineering fees of $300-$1,500 can be a surprise if you did not budget for them.

contractorQuestions.question: What type and size of beam do you plan to use?
contractorQuestions.why: The beam spec should come from the structural engineer, not the contractor's guess. If the contractor quotes a beam size before engineering is done, they may be cutting corners.

contractorQuestions.question: How will you handle temporary shoring during the work?
contractorQuestions.why: Proper temporary support is critical to safety. The contractor should describe their shoring plan clearly - temporary walls or adjustable posts on both sides of the existing wall to hold the load while the beam goes in.

contractorQuestions.question: Are there any utilities in this wall that need rerouting?
contractorQuestions.why: Electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and sometimes gas lines run through interior walls. Rerouting costs can double the project price if not identified upfront.

contractorQuestions.question: Will the beam be concealed or exposed, and what does each option cost?
contractorQuestions.why: Concealing the beam in the ceiling framing costs $800-$1,500 more than leaving it exposed. Knowing the cost difference helps you make a design decision that fits your budget.

secondaryKeywords: cost to remove a load bearing wall
how much to remove a wall
wall removal cost per linear foot
seo.metaDescription: Load-bearing wall removal costs $1,500-$8,000 in 2026, with a national average of $3,500. See cost breakdowns by wall length, beam type, and region, plus use our free calculator.

sources.name: Angi - Cost to Remove a Load-Bearing Wall
